Hi, welcome to video number two in the Living Legend video series. My name is Greg Rowlett and co-host of Celebrity Expert Live with my partner, Nick Nann. How's it going, Nick? Well, man, excited to be here in video two. We got people really educated in video one about kind of what the issue is here with being a nameless, faceless business. And on that one principle we built this agency on and Dan Kennedy's built his entire career on that people by people. So I'm excited to expound on that a little bit and teach just a couple of secrets that we've really helped our clients use to become celebrity experts in our business, which is part of the path in the road to becoming a living legend. Yeah, I love that. Right now we're in our set here that we use for Celebrity Expert Live. And the reason we're doing that is because it really solidifies the main principle of what we're talking about in this video, which is that all legends and all celebrities use media, right? But not everyone knows how to use media the right way. And so that's what this video is going to be explaining. We're going to be going through two fundamental principles to help you become a living legend in your business. And that is the business trifecta and the secret formula for media success. So let's start by talking about this business trifecta and what it means to us as entrepreneurs who are trying to become living legends. Sure. So I say the business trifecta is the equivalent of three cylinders you should be running your business on. And most people know about one or two. They've heard of all three, but they aren't running their business that way. And so I kind of liken it to a story I like to tell when I was 16 and I bought my first car and it was a Daihatsu charade. It was sweet ride. Yeah. Hatchback. And it actually was a three cylinder car. I didn't even know they made three cylinder cars, but it is what it is. So actually we're here in Florida and it gets kind of hot in summer and all year round it can get kind of hot. And so I used to have to turn off the air conditioner to get on the interstate. So dates loved this. They loved you. Absolutely. So well, they loved me. Just not that. So the fact of the matter is that when I was getting an oil change one time and a mechanic is like, hey Nick, did you realize you're only running on two cylinders? I'm like, no idea. He's like, well, I can fix this for you. And so I pay him to fix it. And then what do you know? I can get on the interstate with air conditioning, full blast dates are in heaven. I'm in heaven life's good. So this is the equivalent of what most people are doing in their businesses. They're running on one, maybe two cylinders, not all three. And those three cylinders are simple to say, but harder to figure out how they work together. Those three cylinders are media, marketing and PR. You might want to write those down. Media, marketing and PR. So most people will say, hey Nick, I've tried to use media before and media can be anything and we'll talk a little bit more about this in a bit, but anything from a book to a business card to a brochure to CDs, DVDs, sales letters, whatever it is. It's a medium for telling your story. It's a medium for telling your story. And yeah, one of the first things I say, we're talking about branding here, that branding is simply storytelling. One of the things I don't want you to get confused by is thinking branding is something that doesn't work with direct response marketing or is anything other than telling your story. And media is simply, as you said, a medium for telling your story. So the fact of the matter though is that the media is not going to sprout legs and walk and go tell the world about what you have to do. Most people I know who are just getting started in business or even been started in business or have been in business for years, they'll say something like, Nick, I put out a book and just didn't do anything for me or I got these awesome brochures, but I've still got 3,000 of them in the closet. And it's because it really is only a medium for telling your story, but that's where it starts and ends. Now PR, there's really kind of two types of PR. There's traditional offline PR and there's online PR. They both are great for two different things. Let's focus a little bit on online PR because online PR is the kind of thing you can put out a press release for anything you ever wanted to talk about, syndicated online. We like to use a press release syndication engine called prlog, prlog.org, that's a free one for you. PRlog.org, that's free. If you write a press release and you put your name and your business name in the subject line, the press release or in the title, the header and you syndicate this out, then what happens is it goes out to hundreds of different websites. At the bottom of it, you should have a link to what your business is. When people Google you, they search for you, you know that's what people do, right? The first thing when they meet you, that's the first thing they do, they're going to Google you to figure you out. Now they can do it quick on their phone, right? It's fast. They do it everywhere. The first thing they're going to see is if they see you got an award in 1987, not... It's not super relevant. Not super relevant. If they can't find you, they're going to wonder if you're in the witness protection program. Right. If they can find you, you want to make sure it's something you want them to find. Using a press release and syndicating and not just leaving it on your website or on your desk or on your computer hard drive, syndicating it out on these media websites like PRLog.org, these press release syndication engines. One of the first things that comes up is these press releases on pages. I have clients who have never been on Google before in their life. I mean, they've searched Google, just haven't been a result for themselves or their name. After working with us for a couple of years, they're like dominating three and four pages of it because we have so many press releases out there. Think about things you're doing today that are relevant and put them out there. Press releases and PR are third party validation of what you do and who you say you are. You can tell your own story all you want in media. But PR is what validates. The beauty of press releases is their third party format, but you get to write them. Or you have somebody in your office writing you just to prove them. Our clients, we write them, they just to prove them. So a beautiful thing. So that's what PR really is, is third party validation of who you say you are. That's kind of an example of a press release. It would be like Emmy Award winning Nick Nanton produces Emmy Award winning film. It can be, absolutely. Yes. So a lot of times we'll say like Nick Nanton, founder of the celebrity branding agency, post-new blog on his website. It can be anything. The online press releases you literally can post almost anything. So it doesn't have to be earth shattering news in order to be a press release in this manner. It does not have to be earth shattering news. That's why I like online press releases. The online press releases are great too to use to get in certain newspapers and stuff. You basically write something you think would be newsworthy to a particular news source and you send it out to that news source, that editor, whatever. It just takes a lot more effort than just writing something up yourself and posting it and syndicating it more. So by itself still isn't the end all be all because now we have media and we have kind of a press release. Right. But we're missing the driver of the vehicle. We have what we say our story is and what other people say our story is. But those two things alone will not net you new business typically, right? So that third thing is marketing. Marketing is really the engine that drives it all. It's that one, it's what creates the spark. So if you have, but if you just have marketing by the way, there's a lack of credibility because you're just trying to sell stuff. Right. We'll talk again about that in a second. So media, marketing and PR, these are all three of the things you absolutely must have to have in your business to be driving on all cylinders. And again, we've just touched on them, but hopefully you can get the point of how you've got to use these things together. Just again, one of these things, two of these things way better. But most people would just want these things. People, I hired a PR firm, Nick, I spent $5,000 a month. It just didn't really move the needle for me. They maybe even got you in a big newspaper or on Good Morning America. The fact of the matter is that once those things run in the newspaper or are on TV for that morning, they're never seen again. They're gone forever. They're one and gone, right? Right, right. So that's where the second principle comes into play. And I talk about the secret formula for media success. And this is kind of really where the rubber meets the road. There's two types of media here. There's mass media, television, radio, newspaper, magazines. These things are great for building two things, credibility and awareness, neither of which really generate revenue. Relativity and awareness is great as a part of that formula, but not the entire formula. They don't help feed our kids. They do not help feed our kids. And we both know this very well, right? So the fact of the matter is that you've got mass media. And so again, I made a reference to if someone gets you in a big newspaper or on Good Morning America, I like to tell the story. The very first big PR that I ever got, big media placement, was being in The New York Times. Super excited. I used a service to, I answered a bunch of journalist questions and I finally got in the media. Right. And then the day it ran, I told my mom, told my wife, told my friends. I think my mom, my friends and my wife all told me they saw it. It was great. They bought that paper. They never get that paper, but they bought it today and we saw you, Dick. It was great. And I literally got, besides my inner circle, that new, I literally got one email congratulating me on being in The New York Times. And it was the guy who sold me the frame of me in The New York Times. He sold me the framed article. Now, I did buy it, thankfully, because as you walk into our office now, as you know, there's frame stuff all around the walls in The New York Times, Wall Street Journal, USA Today, Forbes, all this stuff. Which is great. When people walk into our office, we don't have to build our own credibility. The walls literally talk for us. So it's wonderful for that. That's still the credibility, right? And that's not exactly what we're going after. The other thing that I like to like in a two-people go, well, that's kind of weird. And I say to him, well, look, when was the last time you watched Good Morning America? I didn't see it today, did you? Missed it. I'm sure it was great. But when was the last time you watched someone being interviewed on Good Morning America or someone get quoted in a newspaper and you said, that person is so brilliant. I'm going to put this down. I'm going to pause the TV. I'm going to pause the TV. I'm going to go search for that person, find them, and do business with them. It's not what we do. It's very rare. Very rare. Because unless you have like a life-threatening situation and this person is like, they found the cure and it's published there, it's not what we do. We do get educated when we watch TV and read newspapers and magazines and pay attention to mass media. But it's really a form of entertainment that is we're getting educated. And the only thing that really does what I call a pattern interrupt and makes you stop just being in a lull by this media and do something that would be a commercial or direct response commercial and add some sort of thing like that, which are great, but they won't let you do that when you're interviewed. Yeah, it's really when we're watching TV, we're in like a lean back mode. We're not looking forward to doing something, whereas if we're on the computer or doing something more in a direct response, we're leaning forward to take that action. Absolutely. Which brings us to direct media. And again, we talked a little bit about media before. Again, just to meet and for telling you a story, but direct media is the concept that Dan Kennedy talks about all the time of building a herd. We call it building a fan base of gathering and capturing lead information for people who are actually interested in what you have to offer. People actually could take advantage of your services. I always use this example. I've got a client who teaches moms, new moms how to lose the baby fat. So how to lose the weight they gained during pregnancy. And there are many people, including myself, who are not a client for that, right? I mean, I probably could use her stuff. Right. Some of the principles might apply. Right, but I don't need to lose the baby fat, right? So I'm not listening to her message. So even if she was on mass media, I wouldn't be paying attention. She'd be losing my eyeballs and lots of others because they just weren't interested. So the concept of direct media is collecting the names of those people and contacting for attention to those people who would be interested in. So those could be through opt-ins on a website. Those could be through collecting business cards, through just being referred by a friend. But collecting those people and building a list of people who care and then sending out, spending all your time, energy, effort, and money, sending out media that you create to that audience that continues to make offers and tell your story. So that can be, again, everything from sales letters to postcards to magazines, newsletters, books. CDs, DVDs. I mean, the list just goes on and on. Info-proud. So give me anything. Awesome. The best part about direct media is it's really marketing. You're really just, you're going in for the kill. You're going in for the sell. Now the problem there can be the reverse problem of the other one. There's no credibility in it because as soon as someone starts to try to sell you something, you're back right off. You're back right off, right? Because we love to buy and hate to be sold. Everyone knows that, right? So the concept here is for the secret formula for media success, it's very simple. You get the mass media credibility and credentials as fast, quickly, and as inexpensively as you can, and you insert those in your direct media. So I'm going to give you an obnoxious example. I haven't ever done this. It might work. I don't know, but it'll give you the point. So if I sent you a piece of direct mail that you'd never heard of me before, I might say something like, how my name is Nick Nanton. You might have recently read one of my 33 bestselling books alongside Steve Forbes, Jack Canfield, Dan Kennedy, or Brian Tracy. Or perhaps you've seen me in USA Today, Wall Street Journal Newsweek, Ink Magazine or Forge Magazine, or maybe you've seen me on NBC, CBS, ABC Fox, or perhaps in my hit series, you know, I can't remember my series. Proposal success on the biography channel. So it's just so much stuff. And people would say, man, where does this guy been? I must have been living in a vacuum. I must be in a coma. This guy's been everywhere and I have never seen him. And the fact of the matter is you just weren't looking where I was. But the fact of the matter is the only place I really need to be, and we talked a little bit about this in the first video, is where my target clients and prospects are looking. So the fact of the matter is I'm going to go out and try to get this mass media inexpensively and quickly so that I can insert it in my direct media so that when I go out with my direct media, I have credibility and credentials. When people are looking on my website, when they Google me, find me a look on my website, they can see the string of media. Ideally, I'll have tear sheets and all the other stuff to show from it, the interviews if I can get access to it. But really, that's just a huge concept of using mass media credentials and inserting them in your direct media. I have wasted thousands of hours and tens of thousands of dollars myself before I figured that out. And if I added up all the clients that we have, we have over 2,000 clients, that they've all wasted not understanding this concept, it's a shame. I wish I had all that money and we could give it to charity or something because it's just way too much. So just really understand that concept. This is crucial and everything I'm going to teach you is that this is not for ego, this is for building your business and helping more people. That's what it's for. Of course, when you help more people, they help you, you're going to get what you want, whether that's more time with your family or more money or bank account, whatever that is. But if you use this form, then you understand that you want to get media, marketing and PR all working together and you want to use the secret formula for media success, mass media credentials and insert it in your direct media. It changes everything. Now, a quick warning, if you don't have any foundation you've built your business on, if you are scamming people or you're not doing the right thing, you're not following through with your promises, you're giving people bad customer service, anything I can do to build you up is going to crumble very quickly. But if you use these principles and use them to build solid businesses like our clients have done, and we work with some of the biggest players in the Glaser Kennedy world with Dan Kennedy and some of the other people and they've had great thriving businesses, we plug this stuff in and they shoot for the moon because this stuff absolutely works. It's just no one has taken the time to explain it to you in a full amount like this where it makes sense because quite frankly, I love PR firms, I love media and ad agencies, I love all these people but they usually don't do all these things so their only goal is they have a box and you're going to go in the box. Because everyone, if I'm a hammer, everything looks like a man. So we try to have a full toolbox of media, marketing and PR that people can use and then to understand how mass media credentials plays into direct media. Don't fall into the trap. It's much like Dan would talk about image branding. It's not that image branding doesn't work on some level but you've got to have millions and millions of dollars to spend on advertising before you get to a point where image advertising is even worthwhile and even then it's debatable in some cases. But the fact of the matter is just trying to get, spending a bunch of money or time for ego, trying to get on the next news station, the next, I mean if you do it, do it for a purpose and a reason, do not get lost in thinking that you will not have to do marketing. I think the reason why most people like media and PR is because someone will do it for them. And marketing, you can have someone do marketing for you too. There's people who are great at it but it typically costs you more money because it's truly investing in exponential profits. And it's hard sometimes to have the faith and the trust in someone to get them to do that for you or you've got to understand it and it's a little more complex than just, oh I can have some of this for sure. So that's where I see most people, they do two of those things pretty well and then they really want to push the marketing off to someone else, they don't want to understand it and they just really, they have two strategies, hope and pray, which are great strategies for other things. But for this it's not in that you're just sticking your head in the sand and just really hoping it works out and it won't. So if you want to grow your business on all cylinders you've got to have all three and then again, how those two types of media. So if we're not the Florida football team, we're not working on hope and pray this year. Let's say we're a dentist, right? So how do we now put the business track factor and Secret Formula of Media success to work in our business, right? So I guess we start with we have an exceptional service, we do great things for our clients. I mean where do we go from there? So I'll give you an example of Dr. Schuessman. Dr. Schuessman is a client of ours out of Columbus, Ohio and he loves him some Ohio State but we won't go there right now because as you know I'm a Gator fan. But so when you walk into Dr. Schuessman's office he's got framed copies of some of the collaborative books he's done with us and Brian Tracy and others and they're on the wall with the best seller certificate. He's got Quillie awards from the National Academy of Best Selling Authors there around his office. He was in the Inc. 5,000 several times. He's got that framed up. He's been in the USA today, the Wall Street Journal. He's got all these things telling a story. Now when you walk into Dr. Schuessman's office, you're no longer walking into the office of a dentist and the good thing for a professional practice, most people don't think of a professional practice trying to sell them something but they all are. That's how they make money, right? They're selling dentistry work, whatever it is. Right, they're not selling the 2,999 teeth cleaning. They're not with their son. Exactly, they're not with their son. So when you walk in, the good news is you're not even thinking about being sold anything. You're feeling really blessed to be in the company of this celebrity who's willing to help you out with whatever problems you're having with your teeth. You know instantly by the credibility around you that you're working with the best. Now he does a great job too at direct mail and other things, inserting that stuff in his marketing. If you go Google him, you will find his press releases will come up and so he's using that in a dental practice to grow and hit things like the Inc. 5,000 because he understands. First of all, he understands marketing. I think Dan and I would both agree that if you had to do just one of these three things, marketing would absolutely be the one. But you can make your marketing work so much better if you just add media and PR on top of it because it adds that legitimacy and storytelling on top of it. And one of the things that I like to say is that the guy with the best story always wins. And when you're not taking the chance to tell your story, people won't understand what makes sense, why you are where you are, why you came from where you are in order to get to where you are now. And so telling that story in your media allows people to make sense of it and allows them to trust you. Another thing we teach is called personal hooks. So when I tell my story, I talk about how I'm from Barbados, I talk about my kids, I talk about how I'm a Florida Gator. And I've had people walk up to me after my speech and hire me just because they liked Barbados, literally. I've had people spend 20 something thousand dollars on me just because they liked Barbados. Now, I like it too and that's great. So it all worked out. But the fact of the matter is that there were personal hooks in my story that anyone listening could hear what I had to say. And I hopefully threw out enough personal hooks that people who would have like-minded characteristics to me, they would all feel like they had something in common with me. And as we all know from learning from Dan and building trust and everything else, people who have, it creates affinity when you have like characteristics. So I try to throw out as many things as possible so that after I speak, that people will trust me because they have something that they could talk to me about, oh, he has a kid, he's not just some rogue dude running from town to town, you know, skip the country if something goes wrong, right? I'm a family, I'm a member of my church. I try to talk about all these things in order to let people know who I am because when we talked in the first video about people by people and they buy from people they know, like and trust, you got to let them know you're like you, right? You got to let them know- You got to let them in at some level, right? And Dan says it best when he talks about the concept of dog whistles too. A lot of people get afraid to talk about certain things in life, whether it's a divorce or being an alcoholic or whatever it might be. And Dan does a great job of kind of lightly talking about some of those things in his life, but with a serious tone. He talks about the fact that someone who hasn't been through those things will now go, wow, that's crazy, you know, wow, that's intense. Someone who has been through it will say it'll be like a dog whistle, they'll perk up and they'll instantly pay more attention because they know that you are a lot more like them, you understand where they're coming from. So with Dan helping small business owners, he's been through the struggles they've been through and he lets them know that because ultimately if you can let them know that you have been where they are, the biggest problem with someone hiring you, especially if you look like an expert is the fact that they think you're too far removed from where they are. There's no way this person could, you know, understand what I'm going through. Could descend from the mountain top of success to help someone like me. And so one thing we have to do is we have to combat that. So telling your story is huge. And so again, you got to make sure you tell your story and people don't want to know typically the technical things in your business. Yeah. You know, you work with a lot of clients and put them on newsletters and all sorts of stuff and Dan does this too. And Dan says it best that, you know, a great newsletter should be the equivalent of sitting down to have a cup of coffee with a good friend. That's talking about vacations, family, it's talking about lots of things relating it back to business. It can't just be drivel. It's got to relate back to business. But when you can do that, you can create relationships. You can throw out those hooks that the next time someone wants to do business with you, they're going to subconsciously trust you even more just because of those hooks. So again, people buy people, do not forget that. That all relates in. If you do not add your personality into the things we're talking about, the business trifecta and the secret formula for media success, it just won't work. And one of the things that you know, we get objections all the time to people saying, Nick, I don't want to insert myself in my business. Then I may not be able to sell it at some point or I can't get away from my business to go on vacation. All the time. Here all the time. And I would say that that's just a poorly built business, not the fact it's built on your personality. We've seen hundreds and hundreds and hundreds of businesses that are well built, including our business. I'm not in the office most of the time, not because I'm not working, because I'm out working hard somewhere else, either shooting documentaries or out on the road speaking or whatever it might be. And the fact of the matter is that I want my business to run well while I'm not here, but I am one of the faces of the business. I was kind of the first face of the business. Now there's Jack and you and Lindsay and all that. But so the fact of the matter is you can have a celebrity driven, a celebrity branded business and you do not have to be there every day. I want to work past that objection right now. You can become a living legend. And the further you go down that path to being a living legend, the more people will bend to what you tell them to do. There's one reason why people will pay twenty something thousand dollars a day, fight a cold Cleveland to meet with Dan Kennedy for a full day. And when you go to meet with Dan Kennedy for a full day, it's not full of wifi and laptops and cell phones and all these exciting social media things, right? It's a day of grinding it out to grow your business with some hard questions that are hard to answer, right? But we'll do that because Dan is a living legend. Hundreds, thousands of people will pack up every year and go to the super conference and then for some of it because Dan Kennedy is a living legend. When you are a living legend and you use personal branding to get to that level, you get to create the rules, right? The rules like you're not going to have a cell phone and you're only going to take faxes, right? Everybody knows Dan, you know that's the case, right? Yeah, and I mean, really just going back to the business trifecta, Seeker Forming Media says Dan does this with everything that he does. I mean, think about every book that he does. I mean, this guy is putting out two, three books every single year and I know you just finished co-authoring the new brand building book, but so he writes the book, right? And that is the media for telling the story of why you want to use branding and direct response, but then he runs a full page ad in Entrepreneur Magazine, which is the marketing. It's also media, but that lends back into the book. He's also got all the newsletters, right? So he's got the NoBS letter, he's got the Dan Kennedy letter, he's got the Info Marketing letter, all these different newsletters that say, hey, I just wrote this book, go get it, right? So he's using the business trifecta pointing back to that book to get sales and when people buy the book, well now he's got the marketing funnel in place, the journey from a book buyer to a newsletter subscriber to a diamond subscriber to a platinum member, a titanium member, a private buyer. I was going to send you up the ladder, but it all starts by understanding the core principles that we were talking about here in this business is having a media to tell your story and then marketing that story through your marketing and through your PR. It absolutely does. And that's just part of the little engine program. That's like a teeny tiny slice, right? Microcosm. But if you can take that, I mean really understanding that your brand is your story, right? And then you use media to tell that story, which defines your brand. If you start there, if you just understand that part of this process, you're going to be ahead of every business owner in your category. And it's not that these businesses are that different from each other. They are a nuance, but not in all the same things. We all have to have paying customers and retention and customer service and a product service to sell, great offers, value exchange, all these things.
